items.q: Why is injection moulding suited to high-volume production?
items.correct: Tooling cost is high but per-unit cost is very low at scale.
items.misconception: It is the cheapest method for any quantity.
items.random_wrong: It is the only way to make plastic items.
items.why: Students think a 'good' process is good for everything. Injection moulding has expensive moulds - it's only economical when costs are spread over thousands or millions of parts.

items.q: What gives carbon fibre composite its strength?
items.correct: The fibres provide tensile strength; the resin matrix holds them in place and transfers loads.
items.misconception: The carbon fibres alone - the resin is just glue.
items.why: Students dismiss the matrix as filler. In reality the matrix is essential: it transfers loads between fibres and protects them. Loose carbon fibres without matrix have very little structural strength.

items.correct: A small integrated chip containing CPU, memory, and I/O for controlling a device.
items.misconception: A very small computer like a laptop.
items.random_wrong: A remote control for a TV.
items.why: Students think microcontrollers are mini-PCs. They are specialised: limited resources, designed for a single embedded task. No operating system needed - just dedicated code running directly.

items.q: Whose 1955 Designing for People standardised anthropometric data?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dreyfuss. “Loewy” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Fitts' Law. “Hick-Hyman Law” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which percentile range do designs typically accommodate?
items.why: Exact answer: 95 percentile. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
